section.title: Blessed Is the Man Who Fears the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The twin acrostic to Psalm 111, this psalm describes the flourishing, generous, unshaken life that results when a person fears the great God the previous psalm celebrates, closing the pair by contrasting the righteous with the wicked.
section.thesis: The one who fears the LORD and delights in His commands receives an unshaken, generous, and enduring life, a stability that flows not from circumstance but from a heart anchored in God while the schemes of the wicked come to nothing.
section.frameNote: Three movements mirroring the acrostic structure: the foundation of blessing in fearing the LORD, with legacy and provision that follow, verses 1-4; steadfast stability and fearless confidence in the LORD amid bad news, verses 5-9; and the closing contrast with the wicked, whose desires perish, verse 10.

units.label: Blessed is the man who fears the LORD

verses.text: Hallelujah! Blessed is the man who fears the LORD, who greatly delights in His commandments.
His descendants will be mighty in the land; the generation of the upright will be blessed.
Wealth and riches are in his house, and his righteousness endures forever.
Light dawns in the darkness for the upright, for the gracious, compassionate, and righteous.
It is well with the man who is generous and lends freely, whose affairs are guided by justice.
Surely he will never be {{mot|shaken}}; the righteous man will be remembered forever.
He does not fear bad news; his heart is steadfast, trusting in the LORD.
His heart is assured; he does not fear, until he looks in triumph on his foes.
He has scattered abroad his {{tsedaqah|gifts}} to the poor; his righteousness endures forever; his horn will be lifted high in honor.
The wicked man will see and be grieved; he will gnash his teeth and waste away; the desires of the wicked will perish.

mot.gloss: To slip, totter, be shaken loose. The righteous person built on the fear of the LORD is pictured as immovable, not because circumstances never shake, but because the foundation beneath him holds firm.

tsedaqah.gloss: Righteousness, but here specifically expressed as generous giving to the poor. Paul quotes this exact verse, 2 Corinthians 9:9, to root Christian generosity in the same enduring righteousness this psalm describes.

basic.context: The twin of Psalm 111, this acrostic hymn moves from praising the great God to describing the flourishing, generous, and unshaken life of the one who fears Him, closing with a brief but sharp contrast to the wicked, whose hopes come to nothing.
basic.hermeneutics: The blessings described, legacy, wealth, stability, are not a formula guaranteeing prosperity to anyone who merely performs religious duties, but a portrait of the settled confidence and generous character that genuinely flows from a heart anchored in reverent trust of the LORD.
basic.originalLanguages: The righteous man will never be mot, shaken or toppled, verse 6, because his heart, verse 7, is fixed on the LORD rather than on circumstance. His giving is described as tsedaqah, righteousness expressed in generosity, verse 9, a verse Paul later applies directly to Christian giving, 2 Corinthians 9:9.
basic.typology: The steadfast, fearless, generous man of this psalm anticipates Christ, who delighted perfectly in His Father's commandments, John 8:29, brought light into darkness, John 1:4-5, gave generously to the poor, and whose righteousness endures forever without any need of renewal.
basic.christ: Jesus embodies this psalm's portrait perfectly, delighting in His Father's will, John 8:29, and Paul explicitly quotes verse 9, He has scattered abroad his gifts to the poor, his righteousness endures forever, 2 Corinthians 9:9, applying this psalm's generosity to the pattern of Christ, who though rich became poor so that His people might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9.
basic.god: God is the one whose commandments are worth delighting in, whose provision undergirds true wealth and legacy, and who is the steady object of trust that keeps the righteous heart unshaken even when bad news arrives.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Corinthians 9:9 quotes verse 9 directly, applying its language of generous, enduring righteousness to Christian giving. Proverbs 9:10 echoes verse 1's foundation, the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom. Psalm 1 provides a similar contrast between the flourishing righteous and the perishing wicked.
basic.oneStory: The unshaken, generous life described here flows entirely from fearing and delighting in the LORD, a portrait perfectly fulfilled in Christ, whose own righteousness endures forever and whose generosity toward the poor, quoted directly by Paul, becomes the pattern and the power for every believer's steadfast, giving life.

context.literary: An alphabetic acrostic matching Psalm 111 letter for letter, twenty-two half-verses in Hebrew, with a chiastic echo linking the two psalms, the fear of the LORD closing 111:10 and opening 112:1, so that the great God of the first psalm produces the flourishing life of the second.
context.historical: Likely sung alongside Psalm 111 in temple worship, possibly in the early Second Temple period, when covenant faithfulness and the blessings of obedience needed fresh reaffirmation among a restored community.
context.canonical: Echoes Deuteronomy's covenant blessings tied to obedience, Deuteronomy 28:1-14, and anticipates Psalm 1's contrast between the flourishing righteous and the perishing wicked, forming a bridge between wisdom instruction and covenant blessing.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To portray, in vivid and concrete terms, the settled, generous, and unshaken character that genuinely results from fearing the LORD and delighting in His commandments, while briefly contrasting this with the frustrated collapse of the wicked's hopes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes an idealized portrait of covenant blessing following obedience; it prescribes the ongoing pattern of delighting in God's commands, generous giving, and steadfast trust, without promising that every individual believer will experience material wealth as an automatic formula.

debates.question: Does this psalm promise material wealth and prosperity to every person who fears the LORD?

views.label: The psalm describes a general covenantal pattern of blessing, not an ironclad individual guarantee
views.case: Wisdom literature elsewhere, such as Job and Ecclesiastes, shows that the righteous sometimes suffer materially, so this psalm's language functions as a general pattern of blessing under God's moral order rather than a promise applicable in every individual case.

views.label: The psalm's language should be read primarily in terms of true, enduring riches, righteousness and legacy, rather than literal material wealth
views.case: Verse 3 pairs wealth and riches immediately with 'his righteousness endures forever,' suggesting the psalm's ultimate concern is a treasure that outlasts material goods, fulfilled supremely in Christ's giving, 2 Corinthians 8:9.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that fearing the LORD produces a stability and generosity of character that material circumstances cannot ultimately shake or measure.

study.originalLanguages: The righteous man will never be mot, shaken or toppled, verse 6, because his heart is fixed, verse 7, trusting the LORD rather than circumstance. His giving is called tsedaqah, righteousness expressed as generosity toward the poor, verse 9, the very verse Paul quotes to ground Christian giving, 2 Corinthians 9:9.

typology.type: The man whose righteousness endures forever through generous giving, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, who though rich became poor for our sake, so that through His poverty we might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9, the pattern Paul directly links to this verse in 2 Corinthians 9:9.

typology.type: Light dawning in the darkness for the upright, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the true light that dawns in darkness, guiding feet into the way of peace, Luke 1:78-79, John 1:4-5.

study.christ: Jesus perfectly embodies the righteous man of this psalm, delighting greatly in His Father's commandments, John 8:29, and bringing light into darkness, John 1:4-5. Paul quotes verse 9 directly, He has scattered abroad his gifts to the poor, his righteousness endures forever, 2 Corinthians 9:9, applying this psalm's generosity to the gospel pattern of Christ, who though rich became poor so that His people, through His poverty, might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9, and who empowers the same steadfast, generous life in all who trust Him.
study.god: God is the one whose commandments are worth delighting in and whose steady faithfulness undergirds a heart that need not fear bad news. He is the source of true and lasting righteousness, and He ensures that the schemes of the wicked, however threatening, ultimately come to nothing.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes verse 9 directly, applying its language of enduring righteousness expressed in generosity to Christian giving.
Describes Christ becoming poor so that His people might become rich, the gospel pattern behind this psalm's generosity.
Echoes this psalm's foundation, that the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom and blessing.

study.oneStory: The unshaken, generous life this psalm describes flows entirely from fearing and delighting in the LORD, a life perfectly embodied in Christ, whose righteousness endures forever and whose generosity toward the poor, quoted directly by Paul, becomes both the pattern and the power for every believer's steadfast and giving life, while the wicked's every hope finally comes to nothing.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, what marks the blessed man at the foundation of this psalm's portrait?

options.text: He fears the LORD and greatly delights in His commandments.
options.note: This reverent delight, not mere outward performance, is the psalm's starting point.

options.text: He has inherited great wealth from his ancestors.
options.note: The foundation named is fearing the LORD, not inherited wealth, which the psalm mentions only later as a result.

options.text: He has never faced any hardship or bad news.
options.note: The psalm assumes bad news can come, verse 7; the foundation is fearing the LORD despite it.

options.text: He avoids all commandments in favor of personal freedom.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; he greatly delights in God's commandments.

quiz.q: Why, according to verse 7, does the righteous man not fear bad news?

options.text: His heart is steadfast, trusting in the LORD.
options.note: The stability described is rooted in trust, not in the absence of difficult circumstances.

options.text: He never hears any bad news at all.
options.note: The verse assumes bad news can come; his security is in trusting the LORD despite it.

options.text: He has stockpiled enough resources to handle any crisis alone.
options.note: The text credits his steadfast trust in the LORD, not self-reliant stockpiling.

options.text: He is naturally fearless by temperament.
options.note: The confidence described is explicitly rooted in trusting the LORD, not natural temperament.

quiz.q: What does verse 9 say the righteous man has done, and what New Testament passage quotes it directly?

options.text: He has scattered abroad his gifts to the poor, quoted directly in 2 Corinthians 9:9.
options.note: Paul applies this exact verse to the pattern of generous Christian giving.

options.text: He has hoarded his wealth for his own family alone, with no New Testament connection.
options.note: The verse describes generous scattering to the poor, and Paul quotes it directly in 2 Corinthians 9:9.

options.text: He has given only to relatives, quoted in the book of James.
options.note: The giving described is to the poor broadly, and the direct quotation is in 2 Corinthians, not James.

options.text: He has loaned money at high interest, quoted in Romans.
options.note: Verse 5 actually commends fair, generous lending, and the direct quotation of verse 9 is in 2 Corinthians 9:9, not Romans.

quiz.q: How does verse 10 describe the fate of the wicked, in contrast to the righteous?

options.text: They see the righteous man's stability and are grieved, gnashing their teeth as their desires perish.
options.note: This sharp contrast closes the psalm, echoing the pattern of Psalm 1.

options.text: They ultimately receive the same enduring legacy as the righteous.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; their desires perish, unlike the righteous man's enduring legacy.

options.text: They are shown mercy and given a share in the righteous man's wealth.
options.note: The verse describes their grief and the perishing of their desires, not a shared inheritance.

options.text: They remain entirely unaffected by observing the righteous man's life.
options.note: The text says they see and are grieved, indicating they are affected, though negatively, not unaffected.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's portrait of righteousness and generosity connect to Christ?

options.text: Christ, though rich, became poor so His people might become rich, the pattern Paul links directly to this psalm's language of enduring righteousness.
options.note: 2 Corinthians 8:9 and 9:9 together connect this psalm's generosity directly to the gospel pattern of Christ.

options.text: The psalm has no connection to Christ or the New Testament.
options.note: Paul quotes verse 9 directly in 2 Corinthians 9:9, connecting it explicitly to Christian giving rooted in Christ.

options.text: It connects only to Old Testament tithing laws, with no further significance.
options.note: While rooted in Old Testament generosity, the psalm's connection reaches forward specifically to Christ's own self-giving pattern.

options.text: It predicts the exact amount every believer must give financially.
options.note: The psalm describes a character of generosity, not a specific numeric giving formula.

questions: The blessed man delights greatly in God's commandments rather than merely tolerating them. Which of God's commands do you currently find hardest to delight in, and what would help you begin to delight in it?

This psalm says the righteous heart is steadfast and unafraid even when bad news comes, because it trusts the LORD rather than circumstances. What bad news are you currently facing, and where is your heart's true anchor right now?

Verse 9 is quoted by Paul to describe generous Christian giving rooted in Christ's own generosity. Is your own giving currently more like scattering generously or like holding tightly? What would open-handed generosity look like for you this week?

The psalm closes by showing that the wicked's desires ultimately perish, no matter how threatening they appear now. Where do you need to trust that ungodly schemes around you will not have the last word?
Christ became poor so that His people might become rich. How does reflecting on His generosity toward you change the way you view your own resources and possessions this week?

translationNotes.note: Hallelujah transliterates the Hebrew Hallelu Yah, Praise the LORD, opening the psalm just as it closes the acrostic of Psalm 111, forming a deliberate literary bridge between the two twin psalms.
Never be shaken renders mot, a term for slipping or being toppled, describing the righteous man's stability as rooted in trust rather than an absence of hardship.
This verse is quoted verbatim in 2 Corinthians 9:9, where Paul applies its language of enduring righteousness expressed in generosity directly to the pattern of Christian giving.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/112.htm because the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this session. Verified 10 verses in order against the section map bounds Psalm 112:1-10. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed for this specific psalm this session, so sermon status is none.
